channel.title: q-fin.RM updates on arXiv.org
channel.description: q-fin.RM updates on the arXiv.org e-print archive.

item.title: ASRI: An Aggregated Systemic Risk Index for Cryptocurrency Markets
item.description: arXiv:2602.03874v3 Announce Type: replace Abstract: The Aggregated Systemic Risk Index (ASRI) is an interpretable, channel-decomposed measure of crypto-native systemic stress, aggregating four transmission channels -- stablecoin concentration, DeFi liquidity, cross-market contagion, and regulatory opacity -- into a single auditable composite. Methodologically it belongs to the financial-network tradition of connectedness and contagion measurement: the contagion channel is a cross-market connectedness construct, and a Diebold-Yilmaz connectedness series computed on the sub-indices serves as the network benchmark. We evaluate it retrospectively against four crypto crises (Terra/Luna, Celsius/3AC, FTX, SVB); it is not a validated real-time early-warning system. Under autocorrelation-robust (HAC) inference referenced to a finite-sample fixed-$b$ distribution, the three large-signal events are only borderline at the 5% level ($p \approx 0.04$--$0.06$, none surviving Bonferroni); Terra/Luna is clearly non-significant. On a fair-baseline comparison on identical labels, ASRI's day-level discrimination (AUROC 0.866) is indistinguishable on marginal bootstrap intervals from its strongest single channel (Contagion, 0.851) and from the first principal component of its channels (0.858); a paired bootstrap separates them only by a small, structural margin ($\leq 0.016$ AUROC, absent in AUPRC) reflecting ASRI nesting those baselines, not a discriminative gain, and a standalone equity-volatility (VIX) series matches ASRI (0.875). We read the value of aggregation as interpretive -- channel attribution, lead-time, and regime structure in one transparent, reproducible composite -- rather than as superior discrimination, alongside a methodological account of how robust inference reshapes crisis-detection claims.
item.dc:creator: Murad Farzulla, Andrew Maksakov
